nodes.title: Sort a copy so input stays unchanged

anchor.snippet: const arr = nums.slice().sort((a, b) => a - b);

nodes.why: Median needs sorted order, and using `slice()` avoids mutating the original array.
nodes.tradeoff: Uses extra memory, but safer for callers that reuse the input.

nodes.title: Handle empty input explicitly
nodes.why: There is no valid median for an empty list, so returning NaN makes that clear.
nodes.alternative: Throw an error for empty input.
nodes.tradeoff: NaN is non-throwing and predictable, but callers must check for it.

nodes.title: Compute center index once
nodes.why: Both odd and even branches rely on the middle position.
nodes.alternative: Compute middle math separately in each branch.
nodes.tradeoff: One shared value keeps the code shorter and easier to read.

nodes.title: Odd length: return the exact middle value
nodes.why: With odd count, one value sits exactly in the center of sorted data.
nodes.alternative: Use a generic average formula for all cases.
nodes.tradeoff: Explicit branch is simpler and avoids unnecessary arithmetic.

nodes.title: Even length: average the two center values
nodes.why: Even-sized arrays have two middle numbers, so median is their mean.
nodes.alternative: Pick lower or upper middle only.
nodes.tradeoff: Average gives the statistical median, which may be non-integer.
